title: Browser Automation for Team Dashboards With Live Session Context
description: Team dashboards often depend on saved state, filters, and trusted sessions. Learn why local browser automation is more reliable than detached browser runs.
keywords: dashboard browser automation with login state
intro: Dashboard work usually depends on the browser state already present in the operator's session, not just on a page URL and a selector list.
problem: Teams automating dashboards often rebuild filters, workspace selection, and login state in fresh sessions before any useful work begins. That adds friction and weakens reliability.
The actual requirement is usually automation that starts from the same trusted session the team already uses every day.
solution: A local browser runtime keeps automation inside the live browser context that already has the right tabs, state, and permissions.
iatlas-browser is designed for this through its daemon, extension bridge, CLI, and MCP support, while the hosted layer stays focused on public retrieval.
takeaways: Dashboard automation is often a real-session problem.
Local runtime reduces setup cost in browser-heavy operations.
Browser state belongs in the architecture decision.
Hosted APIs should remain for public and remote-safe tasks.
steps: Pick a dashboard task that currently depends on saved browser state.
Attach the local runtime to the trusted browser session.
Use snapshots and browser-aware commands to stabilize the flow.
Turn the stable sequence into a reusable local tool.

title: MCP Browser Tools for QA Investigations in Real Browser Sessions
description: QA investigations improve when assistants can inspect the same live browser session as the tester. Learn where MCP browser tools fit.
keywords: mcp browser tools for qa investigations
intro: A browser-aware assistant is only useful in QA when it can inspect the same page state the tester is already looking at.
problem: Without access to the live browser session, assistants can only reason from screenshots or bug notes and miss the state where the failure appears.
Teams evaluating browser-aware QA tools need a real-session MCP setup, not another disconnected browser sandbox.
solution: A local runtime exposed through MCP lets assistants inspect pages, capture snapshots, and navigate within the same browser context as the tester.
iatlas-browser supports this by sharing one local runtime across CLI, MCP, and the localhost daemon.
takeaways: QA investigations benefit from shared browser context.
MCP is strongest when it points to the live local session.
A shared runtime reduces handoff friction.
Hosted APIs remain for public retrieval only.
steps: Start the local runtime before the QA investigation.
Connect the assistant through the MCP configuration.
Use snapshots and browser-aware tools against the live issue.
Keep public-source lookups on the hosted layer.

title: Local Browser API for Operator Scripts and Stateful Tasks
description: Operator scripts often need a browser API on localhost, not a remote browser service. Learn why local browser control is a cleaner fit.
keywords: local browser api for operator scripts
intro: Scripts that drive browser work are easier to trust when they call a stable local endpoint instead of a remote abstraction that cannot see the real session.
problem: A remote browser layer cannot naturally inherit the active tabs, cookies, and role-specific context the operator already has on the local machine.
The need is usually a predictable local control plane for stateful browser work rather than another hosted dependency.
solution: A localhost browser API keeps stateful execution on the same machine as the browser session and makes it easier to integrate with shell scripts and local services.
iatlas-browser exposes exactly this through its daemon while reserving the hosted API surface for public retrieval.
takeaways: Stateful scripts benefit from a local browser API.
Localhost is a strong control boundary for browser tasks.
The machine holding browser state should run the task.
Hosted APIs solve public retrieval, not session-aware control.
steps: Run the local daemon on the operator machine.
Test the health and command endpoints from a script.
Keep session-aware logic local.
Use hosted APIs only for public stateless work.

title: Hosted Open API for Public Page Analysis and Enrichment
description: Public page analysis usually needs hosted retrieval, not a local logged-in browser. Learn why this belongs on the hosted open API surface.
keywords: hosted open api for public page analysis
intro: Many analysis workflows only need metadata, text, or HTML from public pages, which makes them a hosted retrieval problem rather than a session-aware browser problem.
problem: Teams enriching public pages do not need a browser tied to one operator's session. They need a narrow, predictable interface for public retrieval.
Using local browser automation for this layer makes the system more complex without improving the retrieval model.
solution: A hosted open API keeps public retrieval remote-safe and easy to integrate into enrichment pipelines.
The `miaoda.vip/v1/open` endpoint exists for this while the local runtime remains focused on session-aware browser work.
takeaways: Public page analysis belongs on the hosted layer.
The retrieval interface should stay narrow and predictable.
Local runtime should stay focused on browser-session workflows.
Clear boundaries make the product easier to understand.
steps: Register for a hosted API key.
Send public URLs to `/v1/open` in the needed mode.
Store the response in the analysis workflow.
Escalate to local runtime only when a task becomes session-sensitive.

title: Site Adapters for Repeatable Browser Jobs Across Teams
description: Browser jobs scale better when repeated tasks become site adapters. Learn how reusable commands improve discoverability and execution clarity.
keywords: site adapters for repeatable browser jobs
intro: Repeated browser work becomes easier to scale when it is expressed as named adapters rather than as copied scripts and manual habits.
problem: Without adapters, teams execute the same browser jobs differently and lose track of which ones are safe to host versus which ones depend on local browser state.
This makes the browser layer harder to discover, document, and improve over time.
solution: A site adapter model gives repeated jobs stable names, arguments, and execution mode that can be shared across CLI, MCP, and hosted surfaces.
iatlas-browser already follows this pattern through its site catalog and hosted subset.
takeaways: Adapters scale better than one-off scripts.
Execution mode should be explicit in every adapter.
Hosted adapters are for public remote-safe tasks.
Session-aware adapters should stay local.
steps: Inventory the repeatable browser jobs your teams already use.
Normalize names and arguments for those jobs.
Separate hosted-safe jobs from local-only ones.
Publish the resulting adapter catalog clearly.
